conditions.name: Migraine
conditions.description: Severe recurring headaches often with nausea and light sensitivity. Highly treatable with the right diagnosis and plan.

types.name: Migraine without Aura
types.description: Throbbing headache without preceding sensory disturbances.

types.name: Migraine with Aura
types.description: Visual or sensory disturbances precede the headache.

types.name: Chronic Migraine
types.description: Headaches occurring 15+ days per month for more than 3 months.

treatments.name: Preventive Medications
treatments.description: Daily medications to reduce migraine frequency.

treatments.name: Acute Treatment
treatments.description: Medications taken at onset of migraine.

treatments.name: Botox Injections
treatments.description: For chronic migraine, given every 12 weeks.

howWeHandle.title: Detailed History & Trigger Mapping
howWeHandle.description: Thorough consultation to identify migraine patterns and triggers.

howWeHandle.title: Diagnostic Workup
howWeHandle.description: MRI or blood tests to rule out secondary causes.

howWeHandle.title: Personalised Treatment Plan
howWeHandle.description: Combining preventive and acute treatments tailored to your needs.

howWeHandle.title: Regular Follow-up
howWeHandle.description: Monitor response and adjust medications for maximum reduction.

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 1
recoveryPhases.title: Acute Management

timeline.text: Start acute medications, begin migraine diary

warnings: Avoid medication overuse to prevent rebound headaches

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 2
recoveryPhases.title: Preventive Treatment
warnings: Preventive medications take 6-8 weeks to show full effect

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 3
recoveryPhases.title: Long-term Control
warnings: Do not stop preventive medications abruptly

outcomes.title: 50-70% Reduction
outcomes.description: Significant reduction in migraine frequency with preventive treatment.

outcomes.title: Improved Daily Function
outcomes.description: Return to work and normal daily life.

outcomes.title: Fewer Emergency Visits
outcomes.description: Proper treatment reduces emergency visits.

outcomes.title: Better Quality of Life
outcomes.description: Comprehensive management improves overall wellbeing.

items: Headaches more than 4 times per month
Headache with fever or stiff neck

conditions.ifNotTreated: Untreated migraines can progress to chronic, impacting quality of life and work productivity.

faqs.a: Migraine can be lifelong but often improves with age. With proper treatment most patients achieve significant control.
No definitive cure, but migraines can be well controlled with 50-70% reduction in headache days.
Common triggers include stress, hormonal changes, poor sleep, certain foods, bright lights, and strong smells.
Botox is recommended for chronic migraine (15+ days/month) when two preventive medications have not worked.

conditions.name: Epilepsy
conditions.description: A neurological disorder causing recurrent seizures. Most cases are effectively controlled with modern medications.

types.name: Focal Seizures
types.description: Originate in one area of the brain.

types.name: Unknown Onset Seizures
types.description: Seizures where onset cannot be determined.

treatments.name: Anti-Epileptic Drugs
treatments.description: First-line treatment. Medication depends on seizure type.

treatments.name: EEG Monitoring
treatments.description: Regular EEG to monitor brain activity and treatment response.

treatments.name: Ketogenic Diet
treatments.description: High-fat low-carb diet for drug-resistant epilepsy.

howWeHandle.title: Accurate Seizure Classification
howWeHandle.description: EEG studies to correctly classify seizure type.

howWeHandle.title: Identify Underlying Cause
howWeHandle.description: MRI brain and blood tests to find structural or metabolic causes.

howWeHandle.title: Individualised Medication Plan
howWeHandle.description: AED selection based on seizure type, age, and lifestyle.

howWeHandle.title: Safety Planning
howWeHandle.description: Seizure first aid, driving restrictions, and safety counselling.

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 1
recoveryPhases.title: Diagnosis & Initiation
recoveryPhases.description: Confirming diagnosis and starting first AED.
warnings: Never stop AEDs abruptly

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 2
recoveryPhases.title: Optimisation
recoveryPhases.description: Adjusting medication to achieve seizure freedom.
warnings: Report any rash or mood changes immediately

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 3
recoveryPhases.title: Long-term Management
warnings: AED withdrawal only under close supervision

outcomes.title: 70% Seizure Freedom
outcomes.description: 70% achieve complete seizure control with AED therapy.

outcomes.title: Return to Normal Life
outcomes.description: Seizure-free patients can drive, work, and participate in most activities.

outcomes.title: Reduced Injury Risk
outcomes.description: Effective control reduces seizure-related injuries.

outcomes.title: Improved Mental Health
outcomes.description: Seizure control linked to improved mood and confidence.

items: Two or more seizures without recovery between them

conditions.ifNotTreated: Untreated epilepsy increases risk of injury, SUDEP, cognitive decline, and social isolation.

faqs.q: Can epilepsy go away on its own?
faqs.a: Some childhood syndromes remit with age. Adults seizure-free for 2 years may be considered for AED withdrawal under supervision.
Some syndromes have genetic component but most is not directly inherited.
Driving permitted after seizure-free period (typically 1 year) as per medical guidelines.

faqs.q: What to do during a seizure?
faqs.a: Stay calm, protect from injury, turn on side, do NOT put anything in mouth, time the seizure, call emergency if over 5 minutes.

conditions.name: Stroke
conditions.description: Caused by disrupted blood supply to the brain. Early treatment and rehabilitation significantly improves outcomes.

types.name: Ischaemic Stroke
types.description: Caused by a blood clot blocking an artery. 87% of strokes.

types.name: Haemorrhagic Stroke
types.description: Caused by a ruptured blood vessel.

types.name: TIA
types.description: Stroke symptoms that resolve within 24 hours — a warning sign.

treatments.name: Thrombolysis
treatments.description: IV tPA given within 4.5 hours to dissolve clot.

howWeHandle.title: Emergency Assessment
howWeHandle.description: Rapid CT/MRI to confirm stroke type.

howWeHandle.title: Acute Treatment
howWeHandle.description: Immediate thrombolysis within treatment window.

howWeHandle.title: Cause Investigation
howWeHandle.description: Cardiac monitoring and vascular imaging.

howWeHandle.title: Rehabilitation Planning
howWeHandle.description: Early referral to stroke rehabilitation.

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 1
recoveryPhases.title: Acute (0-7 days)
warnings: Blood pressure should not be lowered too aggressively in acute stroke

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 2
recoveryPhases.title: Subacute (1-3 months)
warnings: Most recovery occurs in first 3 months — intensive rehab is critical

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 3
recoveryPhases.title: Long-term (3 months+)
warnings: Risk of recurrent stroke highest in first 90 days

outcomes.title: 60% Improve with Rehab
outcomes.description: Early rehabilitation significantly improves functional outcomes.

outcomes.title: 80% Strokes Preventable
outcomes.description: Addressing risk factors prevents up to 80% of strokes.

outcomes.title: Independence Regained
outcomes.description: Many survivors regain ability to walk and perform daily activities.

outcomes.title: Recurrence Reducible
outcomes.description: Secondary prevention reduces risk of second stroke by 80%.

whenToSeeDoctor.intro: FAST — call emergency services immediately for:

conditions.ifNotTreated: Stroke left untreated causes irreversible brain damage. Every minute causes death of 1.9 million neurons.

faqs.q: What is the difference between stroke and TIA?
faqs.a: TIA causes stroke-like symptoms that fully resolve within 24 hours. It is a serious warning sign.

faqs.q: How long does stroke recovery take?
faqs.a: Most recovery in first 3-6 months. Improvement can continue for 1-2 years with rehabilitation.

faqs.q: Can a young person have a stroke?
faqs.a: Yes. Young adults can have strokes from cardiac conditions, clotting disorders, or illicit drug use.
Post-stroke depression affects up to 33% of survivors and can impair recovery. We screen and treat depression as part of stroke care.

conditions.name: Parkinson's Disease
conditions.description: A progressive movement disorder causing tremors and stiffness. Managed effectively with medications and therapy.

types.name: Idiopathic Parkinson's
types.description: Most common — cause unknown, good response to levodopa.

types.name: Parkinson's Plus Syndromes
types.description: Related conditions that respond less to levodopa.

treatments.name: Levodopa / Carbidopa
treatments.description: Gold standard treatment for Parkinson's motor symptoms.
items: Available in immediate and controlled-release forms

treatments.name: Dopamine Agonists
treatments.description: Mimic dopamine effects. Used in early or younger-onset Parkinson's.

treatments.name: Deep Brain Stimulation
treatments.description: Surgical implantation of electrodes for advanced Parkinson's.

howWeHandle.title: Confirm Diagnosis
howWeHandle.description: Careful clinical assessment to distinguish Parkinson's from other conditions.

howWeHandle.title: Personalise Medication Timing
howWeHandle.description: Optimise levodopa dosing to maximise on time and minimise off periods.

howWeHandle.title: Multidisciplinary Referrals
howWeHandle.description: Coordinate physiotherapy, speech therapy, and occupational therapy.

howWeHandle.title: Advance Planning
howWeHandle.description: Discuss disease progression, driving, and advance care planning.

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 1
recoveryPhases.title: Early Stage
recoveryPhases.description: Diagnosis and initiation with good symptom control.
warnings: Do not start levodopa too late

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 2
recoveryPhases.title: Middle Stage
warnings: Falls risk increases — physiotherapy essential

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 3
recoveryPhases.title: Advanced Stage
warnings: Some Parkinson's drugs worsen confusion in advanced disease

outcomes.title: Maintained Independence
outcomes.description: Good medication management maintains independence for many years.

outcomes.title: 60% DBS Improvement
outcomes.description: DBS provides 60% improvement in motor symptoms.

outcomes.title: Better Quality of Life
outcomes.description: Multidisciplinary care improves quality of life.

outcomes.title: Caregiver Support
outcomes.description: We provide education and support to families and caregivers.

whenToSeeDoctor.intro: See a neurologist if you notice:
items: Resting tremor in hand or leg

conditions.ifNotTreated: Without treatment Parkinson's progresses and motor symptoms become increasingly disabling.

faqs.q: Does everyone with Parkinson's get dementia?
faqs.a: About 30-40% develop Parkinson's dementia in later stages.
Evidence suggests regular vigorous exercise may have neuroprotective effects.
DBS is considered for advanced Parkinson's with significant motor fluctuations not controlled by medications.
Most is not inherited. About 10-15% have a genetic cause.

procedures.name: EEG Test
procedures.shortDescription: Records electrical activity in the brain to diagnose epilepsy, sleep disorders, and neurological conditions.
procedures.candidacyIntro: An EEG is recommended for patients with:

howItWorks.title: Electrode Placement
howItWorks.description: 20-25 small electrodes attached to scalp using conductive gel. No needles.

howItWorks.title: Brain Activity Recording
howItWorks.description: EEG machine records electrical signals for 30-60 minutes.

howItWorks.title: Activation Procedures
howItWorks.description: Hyperventilation and photic stimulation to provoke abnormal activity.

howItWorks.title: Analysis
howItWorks.description: Recording analysed by neurologist and detailed report provided.

howWeHandle.title: Pre-test Instructions
howWeHandle.description: Advise on hair washing and caffeine avoidance.

howWeHandle.title: Comfortable Environment
howWeHandle.description: Quiet relaxed environment for best quality recording.

howWeHandle.title: Expert Interpretation
howWeHandle.description: All EEGs reported by consultant neurologist.

howWeHandle.title: Clear Communication
howWeHandle.description: Results communicated clearly with implications for management.

recoveryPhases.label: During
recoveryPhases.title: During the Procedure
warnings: Inform us if you had a seizure in past 24 hours

recoveryPhases.label: After
recoveryPhases.title: After the Procedure
warnings: If sleep deprivation was used arrange transport home

successRateItems: Normal EEG does not rule out epilepsy
risks: Rare seizure provocation during photic stimulation

outcomes.title: Accurate Diagnosis
outcomes.description: EEG is the cornerstone of epilepsy diagnosis.

outcomes.title: Treatment Monitoring
outcomes.description: Serial EEGs assess response to medications.

outcomes.title: Rule Out Mimics
outcomes.description: EEG distinguishes epileptic seizures from other conditions.

misconceptions.fact: EEG only records electrical brain waves.
EEG is completely painless — no needles or currents.
Up to 50% of epilepsy patients have normal routine EEG.

faqs.q: Do I need to stop medications before EEG?
faqs.a: Usually no — continue medications unless specifically asked to hold them.

faqs.q: What if EEG is normal but I have seizures?
faqs.a: Normal EEG does not rule out epilepsy. Sleep-deprived or ambulatory EEG may be ordered.
Wash hair, avoid caffeine, no hair products, eat normally.

procedures.name: Nerve Conduction Study
procedures.shortDescription: Measures how quickly electrical signals move through nerves to detect neuropathy and nerve damage.
procedures.candidacyIntro: NCS is recommended for patients with:
candidacy: Numbness, tingling, or burning in hands or feet

howItWorks.title: Electrode Placement
howItWorks.description: Surface electrodes placed on skin over the nerve being tested.

howItWorks.title: Electrical Stimulation
howItWorks.description: Small electrical impulse delivered along the nerve — feels like a brief tap.

howItWorks.title: Signal Recording
howItWorks.description: Speed and strength of nerve signal recorded.

howItWorks.title: EMG if required
howItWorks.description: Thin needle into muscles to assess electrical activity.

howWeHandle.title: Targeted Study Design
howWeHandle.description: Tailor NCS protocol to your symptoms.

howWeHandle.title: Patient Comfort
howWeHandle.description: Explain each step and minimise discomfort.

howWeHandle.title: Combined NCS/EMG
howWeHandle.description: Combine with EMG for comprehensive assessment.

howWeHandle.title: Immediate Discussion
howWeHandle.description: Findings discussed immediately after test.

recoveryPhases.label: During
recoveryPhases.title: During the Procedure
recoveryPhases.description: Mild stimulation — most find it tolerable.
warnings: Inform us if you have a pacemaker before EMG

recoveryPhases.label: After
recoveryPhases.title: After the Procedure
warnings: Minor muscle soreness after EMG may last 1-2 days

successRateItems: Gold standard for carpal tunnel diagnosis

outcomes.title: Precise Diagnosis
outcomes.description: Identifies type, location, and severity of nerve damage.

outcomes.title: Treatment Planning
outcomes.description: Results guide surgical or medication decisions.

outcomes.title: Monitoring Progress
outcomes.description: Serial studies monitor recovery from nerve injuries.

misconceptions.fact: Most describe it as a mild shock — brief and tolerable.

faqs.a: The stimulation causes brief mild shock — most find it tolerable.
Keep skin clean, avoid lotions, wear loose clothing.
Carpal tunnel, peripheral neuropathy, Guillain-Barre, motor neuron disease, and nerve injuries.

procedures.name: Botox for Migraine
procedures.shortDescription: Preventive Botox injections reduce the frequency and severity of chronic migraines with long-lasting results.
procedures.candidacyIntro: Botox for migraine is recommended for:
candidacy: Chronic migraine: 15+ headache days per month
At least 2 preventive medications tried and failed

howItWorks.title: Mapping Injection Sites
howItWorks.description: 31 injection sites mapped across forehead, temples, neck, and shoulders.

howItWorks.title: Preparation
howItWorks.description: Skin cleaned. Fine-needle syringes prepared.

howItWorks.title: Injections
howItWorks.description: Small amounts of botulinum toxin injected at each of 31 sites.

howItWorks.title: Post-injection Review
howItWorks.description: Results assessed at follow-up 12 weeks later.

howWeHandle.title: Eligibility Assessment
howWeHandle.description: Review migraine history and confirm criteria.

howWeHandle.title: Personalised Injection Plan
howWeHandle.description: Follow PREEMPT protocol of 31 sites.

howWeHandle.title: Headache Diary Review
howWeHandle.description: Review diary at each visit to quantify response.

howWeHandle.title: Combined Treatment Strategy
howWeHandle.description: Botox as part of comprehensive migraine management.

recoveryPhases.label: Cycle 1
recoveryPhases.title: First Treatment
recoveryPhases.description: Some respond from first cycle; others need 2-3 cycles.
warnings: Do not rub injection sites for 24 hours

recoveryPhases.label: Cycle 2-3
recoveryPhases.title: Building Response
warnings: Keep headache diary to track response

recoveryPhases.label: Maintenance
recoveryPhases.title: Ongoing Treatment
warnings: Botox effect wears off if stopped

successRateItems: Average reduction of 8-9 headache days per month
Approved by NICE for chronic migraine
risks: Bruising at injection sites (common, resolves quickly)
sideEffects: Mild headache on day of treatment

outcomes.title: 50% Fewer Migraine Days
outcomes.description: Most patients experience 50% reduction after 2-3 cycles.

outcomes.title: Reduced Medication Use
outcomes.description: Fewer migraines means less need for acute pain medications.

outcomes.title: Improved Daily Functioning
outcomes.description: Fewer missed work days and better social participation.

misconceptions.fact: It is a licensed medical treatment approved by NICE for chronic migraine prevention.
Takes 2-4 weeks to start working, full effect over 2-3 cycles.

faqs.q: How many sessions will I need?
faqs.a: At least 2-3 cycles (6-9 months) to assess full response.

faqs.q: Will Botox completely stop my migraines?
faqs.a: Complete freedom is uncommon. Goal is 50%+ reduction.

faqs.q: Is it the same as cosmetic Botox?
faqs.a: Same medication but completely different technique, sites, and doses.

faqs.q: Can I have it if pregnant?
faqs.a: Not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding.

procedures.name: Stroke Rehabilitation Therapy
procedures.shortDescription: Structured therapy to restore movement, speech, and cognitive function after a stroke.
candidacy: Any stroke survivor with residual neurological deficits
Weakness or paralysis in arm or leg

howItWorks.title: Neurological Assessment
howItWorks.description: Detailed assessment of all deficits to design personalised rehab plan.

howItWorks.title: Physiotherapy
howItWorks.description: Targeted exercises to regain strength, balance, and walking.

howItWorks.title: Speech & Language Therapy
howItWorks.description: Treatment of aphasia and dysphagia.

howItWorks.title: Occupational Therapy
howItWorks.description: Retraining daily living skills and home modifications.

durationMilestones.description: Functional gains in walking and communication
Return to community and driving assessment

howWeHandle.title: Early Initiation
howWeHandle.description: Rehabilitation within 24-48 hours of stroke.

howWeHandle.title: Multidisciplinary Team
howWeHandle.description: Coordinate PT, OT, speech therapy, neuropsychology.

howWeHandle.title: Goal-Oriented Therapy
howWeHandle.description: Goals set collaboratively with patient and family.

howWeHandle.title: Carer Training
howWeHandle.description: Train family members in safe handling and exercise programmes.

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 1
recoveryPhases.title: Acute Rehabilitation
warnings: Rest is as important as therapy in acute phase

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 2
recoveryPhases.title: Active Recovery
warnings: Spasticity needs early treatment to prevent contractures

recoveryPhases.label: Phase 3
recoveryPhases.title: Community Integration
warnings: Post-stroke depression is common — seek help early

successRateItems: 80% with aphasia show meaningful language improvement

outcomes.title: Return to Walking
outcomes.description: 60-70% regain ability to walk.

outcomes.title: Communication Restored
outcomes.description: Speech therapy significantly improves language.

outcomes.title: Independence Regained
outcomes.description: OT helps relearn daily living skills.

outcomes.title: Community Reintegration
outcomes.description: Return to social life and work.

misconceptions.fact: Improvement continues for years with continued therapy.
Many survivors achieve significant recovery with intensive rehabilitation.

faqs.a: As soon as patient is medically stable — ideally within 24-48 hours.
Most active rehabilitation in first 3-6 months but improvement continues for 1-2 years.
Difficulty with language caused by stroke. Speech therapy significantly improves aphasia.

faqs.q: Can I drive again after stroke?
faqs.a: Depends on deficits. Formal driving assessment required. Many survivors return to driving.

faqs.q: What if seizures are not controlled?
faqs.a: We conduct drug-resistant epilepsy workup and may refer for video EEG or surgical evaluation.
AED withdrawal considered after 2 years seizure-free, done gradually under supervision.

faqs.q: Is this suitable with no symptoms?
faqs.a: Yes — designed for preventive assessment especially with risk factors or family history.
Approximately 2-3 hours including consultation, cognitive testing, ECG, and blood draw.
Cognitive screening identifies early cognitive changes. It is a screening tool — not a definitive diagnostic test.
